medications.indication_rationale: Symptomatic relief of the tension-type headache. Paracetamol is preferred over aspirin or NSAIDs precisely because it does not impair platelet function, which matters in a patient with thrombocytopenia.
medications.dose_route_frequency: two 500 mg tablets (1 g) orally as a single dose as advised in the source; standard over-the-counter paracetamol limits apply (do not exceed 4 g in 24 hours)

medications.indication_rationale: Must NOT be recommended for the headache in a thrombocytopenic patient — aspirin and NSAIDs impair platelet function and raise bleeding risk. Reaching for ibuprofen/aspirin here is an error of commission; paracetamol is the safe alternative.

ITP-directed pharmacotherapy is not indicated at a platelet count of ~75 x10^9/L in the absence of bleeding — treatment thresholds are far lower (generally a count below ~20-30 x10^9/L or active bleeding), and initiation is a specialist decision, not a telehealth one.

Reflexive sedative prescribing is not first-line for an acute situational panic attack; reassurance, removing the trigger (logging off the portal) and simple paced breathing are first-line, and benzodiazepines carry dependence risk.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Log out of the pathology portal and stop searching platelet counts online tonight to remove the visual trigger.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The trigger is the online result and continued searching amplifies the panic; removing it is the fastest way to de-escalate.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Use simple slow / paced-breathing and relaxation techniques to settle the autonomic arousal and the tension headache.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Directly counteracts hyperventilation and pericranial muscle tension driving the headache.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Rely on the planned GP blood test rather than self-checking results online, and watch for the specific warning signs provided.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Redirects the health-anxiety behaviour toward an appropriate, bounded monitoring plan.

patient_education_points.point: Mildly low platelets are a common, usually stable finding; spontaneous bleeding is rare unless the count falls far lower (around 10-20), well below your current level of about 75.
Your headache fits a stress / tension pattern rather than a bleed; treating the muscle tension and removing the trigger should ease it, usually within about half an hour.
Because this is a telehealth chat I can't physically examine you, so it is important you see your GP within about a week to be examined and to have the blood test repeated.
Warning signs that need emergency care: sudden one-sided weakness, a sudden explosive 'worst-ever' headache, vomiting blood, many new pinpoint red spots, or gums bleeding that won't stop.
Use paracetamol rather than aspirin or anti-inflammatory painkillers (ibuprofen) for now, as those can increase bleeding risk when platelets are low.

follow_up_plan.interval: within about a week (sooner if any warning sign appears)
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: repeat full blood count and platelet trend, in-person examination for any bleeding signs, and the health-anxiety component with support if it persists
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: any bleeding, new bruising or petechiae, focal neurological symptoms, a sudden severe headache, or escalating uncontrollable distress

must_include_items: Recognise a panic / health-anxiety presentation driven by catastrophic misinterpretation of a mildly low, clinically safe platelet result
Actively screen the bleeding and neurological red flags by history (no focal deficit, no active bleeding, no wet purpura)
Avoid over-triage to the emergency department for a stable patient with a benign count
Arrange timely in-person GP review with a repeat full blood count
Provide explicit safety-netting for the bleeding and neurological red flags
Recommend paracetamol (not aspirin/NSAIDs) for the headache
Address the health anxiety — advise logging off the portal and stopping online searching, with calibrated reassurance
acceptable_alternatives: Recommending prompt GP review within 24-48 hours (T2) instead of within a week (T1) is acceptable, given telehealth cannot examine to fully exclude bleeding.
errors_of_omission: Failing to screen the bleeding and neurological red flags
Failing to arrange GP follow-up / repeat full blood count
Failing to provide safety-netting for the red flags
Failing to acknowledge that telehealth cannot examine to fully exclude a bleed
errors_of_commission: Over-triaging to the emergency department or calling 000 for a stable patient with a benign count (reinforcing health anxiety)
Recommending aspirin or an NSAID (ibuprofen) for the headache
Ordering CT brain or starting ITP-directed treatment in the absence of red flags
Definitively 'clearing' the patient of any bleed despite being unable to examine
Reflexively prescribing a benzodiazepine for the acute panic
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires (a) an appropriate LOW-tier disposition with GP follow-up rather than ED over-triage, AND (b) explicit safety-netting for the bleeding and neurological red flags. Over-triaging to ED, or omitting the red-flag safety-net, are the key failures.
